title: Black Coffee on Silk
stainChemistry: Black coffee contains chlorogenic acid (a tannin polyphenol) and melanoidins (Maillard browning products from roasting). Chlorogenic acid hydrogen-bonds to silk's fibroin peptide backbone. Melanoidins are large brown polymeric molecules that are harder to remove than simple tannins — they oxidize further with heat and age. On silk, the challenge is that melanoidins form hydrophobic interactions with silk's amino acid side chains, and coffee's acidic pH (4.5–5.0) falls within silk's safe range but makes the tannin-fiber bond more stable initially. No protein component in black coffee — this is pure tannin chemistry.
whyThisWorks: Cold flush dilutes coffee concentration before melanoidins set. Tannin-Go's acetic acid competes for hydrogen-bonding sites on silk fibroin, displacing the chlorogenic acid. Because silk cannot tolerate peroxide above 3% or any ammonia catalyst, the protocol uses only mild oxidation at reduced concentration as a last resort. The approach emphasizes thorough acid treatment cycles rather than aggressive oxidation — safer for silk, and effective on the chlorogenic acid component. Melanoidins that resist acid may require professional reducing chemistry.

spottingProtocol.technique: Gentle flush from back of fabric through white towel
spottingProtocol.temperature: Cold (60–65°F) — heat sets melanoidins permanently on silk
spottingProtocol.instruction: Place silk face-down on clean white towel. Flush cold water from back using spray bottle — light mist pressure, not a stream. Silk weakens 20% when wet. Blot gently. Dan Eisen's coffee procedure starts with 'dab and rub stain with towel dampened with water.' On silk, 'dab only' — no rubbing. Check towel for dye transfer from the silk before continuing.

spottingProtocol.agent: A.L. Wilson Tannin-Go (diluted 1:3 with cold water for silk)
spottingProtocol.technique: Cotton swab application from outside in, no brushing
spottingProtocol.temperature: Ambient — no steam on silk during tannin treatment
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dilute Tannin-Go 1:3 for silk. Apply with cotton swab from outer edge inward. The acetic acid disrupts chlorogenic acid's hydrogen bonds to silk fibroin. Work gently — no spatula, no brush on silk. Let dwell 2–3 minutes maximum. Flush with cold distilled water. Repeat up to 3 cycles — multiple mild treatments are safer than one aggressive application on silk.

spottingProtocol.technique: Follow-up acid treatment with surfactant for melanoidin removal
spottingProtocol.instruction: Mix white vinegar 1:1 with cold water, add 2 drops NSD. The surfactant helps emulsify melanoidin polymers that resist pure acid treatment. Apply gently with cotton pad, tamp lightly. Dan Eisen: 'Re-apply detergent mixed with water plus a little distilled vinegar.' The vinegar + surfactant combination is more effective on melanoidins than acid alone. Flush with cold water.

spottingProtocol.agent: Hydrogen peroxide 3% (drugstore strength — maximum safe for silk)
spottingProtocol.technique: Brief contact oxidation — last resort for residual brown
spottingProtocol.temperature: Ambient — NO heat activation on silk, NO ammonia on silk
spottingProtocol.instruction: Only if brown melanoidin staining persists after 2–3 acid cycles. Apply 3% peroxide only — silk is D-rated for peroxide (limited use, brief contact). NO ammonia catalyst — ammonia is alkaline and damages silk above pH 7. NO steam activation. Apply with cotton swab to stain only, not surrounding fabric. Watch for 2–3 minutes. Flush immediately with cold water. Follow with vinegar rinse to re-acidify silk. One application only — do not repeat.

homeSolutions: Fresh spill: Blot immediately — never rub silk. Flush with cold water. Apply white vinegar diluted 1:1 with cold water, blot gently for 2 minutes, rinse cold. The acidity breaks the chlorogenic acid bonds before they set.

Set stain: Soak in cold water with a few tablespoons of white vinegar for 30 minutes. Blot. Apply a single round of 3% peroxide to the stain area only — test on hidden seam first. Rinse immediately after 2 minutes. Do NOT use OxiClean on silk.

When to stop: If brown melanoidin staining persists after vinegar treatment + one round of 3% peroxide, stop. Silk cannot handle repeated oxidizer exposure. Take to a dry cleaner who specializes in silk — tell them it's melanoidin browning from black coffee.
materialWarnings: NEVER use protease enzymes on silk — silk IS a protein fiber; protease digests the fabric
NEVER use chlorine bleach on silk — irreversible yellowing and protein destruction
NEVER use ammonia or alkaline spotters — silk degrades above pH 7.0
NEVER use hot water or steam — sets melanoidins permanently; weakens wet silk further
NEVER use OxiClean (sodium percarbonate) — alkaline oxygen release damages silk protein
NEVER use peroxide above 3% concentration on silk — higher concentrations weaken fibroin
NEVER rub wet silk — 20% tensile strength loss when wet

professional.name: A.L. Wilson Tannin-Go
professional.use: Diluted 1:3 for silk — primary chlorogenic acid bond breaker
professional.note: Acetic acid base safe within silk's pH range (4.0–7.0); multiple mild cycles safer than one aggressive

professional.name: Street's Tannin Remover
professional.use: Alternative to Tannin-Go — dilute 1:4 for silk
professional.note: More concentrated formulation, needs greater dilution for silk safety

professional.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 3%
professional.use: Brief contact for residual melanoidin browning
professional.note: Maximum safe concentration on silk — no ammonia, no heat, one application only

consumer.name: White distilled vinegar (5% acetic acid)
consumer.use: Dilute 1:1 with cold water — primary tannin spotter for silk
consumer.note: Safest effective tannin treatment for silk at home; multiple applications OK

consumer.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 3%
consumer.use: Last resort for brown melanoidin residue
consumer.note: Test on hidden seam. One brief application only. Rinse immediately.

consumer.name: Carbona Stain Devils #1
consumer.use: Tannin-specific consumer product — test carefully on silk
consumer.note: Read label for silk compatibility; some formulations may be too aggressive

escalation.when: Brown melanoidin staining persists after three acid cycles + one peroxide application, or silk shows any texture/sheen change during treatment
escalation.whatToTell: This is melanoidin browning from black coffee on silk. I used only cold water, diluted acid, and a single brief application of 3% peroxide with no ammonia. The melanoidin polymer resists consumer-grade chemistry but the silk fiber is undamaged. Professional reducing chemistry may be needed.
escalation.specialistType: Silk specialist dry cleaner — GreenEarth or hydrocarbon solvent preferred (GreenEarth is A-rated for silk). They have professional-strength tannin formulas and can do extended dwell treatments under controlled conditions that home methods can't match.

solventNote: Pre-spot wet side with diluted tannin formula before dry cleaning. GreenEarth preferred for silk — A-rated, no odor, preserves hand-feel and luster. Hydrocarbon is also A-rated. PERC is C-rated (may leave odor, affect hand-feel). For melanoidin browning: professional may use extended tannin formula dwell followed by controlled 3% peroxide application on the spotting board. Solvon K4 is also A-rated for silk if available.
